title: Wound Irrigation and Cleansing
subtitle: The nurse irrigates a wound safely — using warmed normal saline, sterile technique for a surgical wound, cleaning from the cleanest to the dirtiest area at safe pressure until the return runs clear, then assessing the wound.

patient.name: Wound Irrigation
patient.admitDate: The nurse cleanses a wound by irrigation to remove debris and promote healing.

nursesNote: The nurse irrigates a wound to remove debris, exudate, and bacteria and to promote healing. Warmed normal saline is the standard irrigant because it is isotonic and does not harm healthy tissue; warming improves comfort and avoids cooling the wound bed. Sterile technique is used for a fresh surgical wound, while clean technique may be used for a chronic wound. After hand hygiene and donning gloves, gown, and goggles, the nurse positions the patient so the solution flows from the cleanest area toward the dirtiest and drains into a basin. A 30-35 mL syringe with a 19-gauge catheter delivers a safe irrigation pressure (about 4-15 psi) that cleans the wound without driving bacteria deeper or damaging tissue; the tip is held about an inch above the bed and not touched to the wound. Irrigation continues until the return solution runs clear, then the nurse pats the area dry, assesses the wound bed for size, color, drainage, odor, and tunneling, and applies the ordered dressing. Foul odor, increasing redness, or purulent drainage is reported.
providerOrders: Irrigate the wound with warmed normal saline
Use sterile technique for the surgical wound and don PPE
Cleanse from the cleanest area toward the dirtiest until the return is clear
Use a 30-35 mL syringe with a 19-gauge catheter for safe pressure
Assess and document the wound; report signs of infection

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about wound irrigation?

opts.text: Irrigation removes debris and exudate to promote healing
Normal saline is the preferred irrigating solution
The wound is irrigated from the cleanest area toward the dirtiest
Forceful high-pressure irrigation is best to blast tissue away
Sterile technique is used for a fresh surgical wound
Irrigation continues until the return solution runs clear
Isotonic saline cleans without harming healthy tissue
A 30-35 mL syringe with a 19g catheter gives safe pressure
Warming the solution improves comfort and supports healing
Irrigating from dirty to clean reduces contamination
Excessive pressure can drive bacteria deeper and harm tissue
Foul odor and purulent drainage suggest infection
Perform hand hygiene, gather supplies, and don PPE
Position the patient so solution drains into a basin
Fill the syringe and irrigate from clean to dirty
Continue until the return solution runs clear
Pat dry, assess the wound, and apply the dressing
Using warmed normal saline to irrigate
Irrigating from the dirtiest area toward the cleanest
Holding the tip about 1 inch above the wound bed
Using forceful high pressure on the wound
Positioning so solution drains into a basin
Reusing the solution that pooled in the wound
Wearing goggles and a gown for splash protection
Skipping wound assessment after irrigation

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The wound now has foul odor, increased redness, and purulent drainage
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the wound and surrounding skin
Obtain vital signs and check for fever
Notify the provider and obtain a wound culture if ordered
Carry out updated irrigation and dressing orders
Document the findings
Using warmed normal saline to irrigate
Irrigating from the dirty area toward the clean area
Irrigating until the return solution runs clear
Using high forceful pressure on the wound bed
Wearing goggles and a gown for splashes
Letting the irrigation tip touch the wound bed
Assessing and documenting the wound afterward
Reusing pooled solution to irrigate again
